title: Pulled Pork
subtitle: Slow-roasted pork shoulder with a dry rub, cooked until it falls apart
description: Pulled pork is slow-cooked pork shoulder, rubbed in spices and cooked at a low temperature for six to eight hours until the collagen in the shoulder has broken down completely and the meat pulls apart in long fibres. The oven version used here produces results close to a true smoke-pit cook without specialist equipment. The dry rub is the flavour layer; the long, low cook is the technique. It is an ideal weekend cook-ahead — the pork improves overnight and reheats perfectly.
sourceNotes: Pulled pork is a cornerstone of American barbecue tradition, with deep roots in the Carolinas and the Southern United States. The dry-rub and low-slow oven method follows the technique described in Steven Raichlen's The Barbecue Bible (1998) and is the standard home-cook adaptation of the pit-smoked original.

recipeTools.notes: With a rack if possible — elevating the shoulder allows air to circulate underneath.
The shoulder is tented halfway through cooking.
Or disposable gloves — for shredding the rested pork.
Target 95 °C internal — at this temperature the collagen has fully converted to gelatin and the pork will pull easily.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mixture of dry spices applied to the surface of meat before cooking. The rub penetrates the outer layer as the meat cooks, forming a flavourful crust called the bark.

The connective tissue in heavily worked muscles like pork shoulder. At temperatures above 85 °C and with sufficient time, collagen converts to gelatin, making the meat moist, rich, and able to be pulled into fibres.

ingredients.notes: Bone-in keeps it moister — about 2.5 kg. Can also use boneless but reduce cook time slightly.
Splashed into the tin to keep the base moist during cooking

steps: Combine all the dry rub ingredients. Pat the pork shoulder dry with kitchen paper. Rub the spice mixture all over every surface, pressing it into any folds or crevices. If time allows, refrigerate uncovered for 4–12 hours to let the rub penetrate.
When ready to cook, remove the pork from the fridge 1 hour before cooking. Heat the oven to 150 °C fan (170 °C / gas 3).
Place the pork fat-side up on a rack in a roasting tin. Pour the apple cider vinegar into the base of the tin.

Cover the tin tightly with foil and roast for a further 3–4 hours until a thermometer reads 95 °C at the thickest point. The collagen will have converted to gelatin at this temperature — the meat will feel very soft when pressed.
Remove from the oven. Leave the pork covered and rest for at least 30 minutes.

Pull the pork: use two forks or hands (protected with disposable gloves) to pull the meat into fibres, discarding any large pieces of fat or bone. Season the pulled meat with any resting juices and a pinch of salt if needed.
Serve in bread rolls with coleslaw, or as part of a larger spread.

content.text: The target internal temperature is 95 °C: not the 70–74 °C used for food safety, which any thermometer reading will confirm long before the pork is ready to pull. At 95 °C, the

in the shoulder has converted to gelatin and the fibres separate without any effort. Below this temperature, the pork is cooked but not pullable — it will shred into dry, tight chunks rather than long, moist fibres.
ingredients. Pat {{pork-shoulder-bone-in}} of pork dry and rub the spices all over every surface. Refrigerate uncovered for up to 12 hours if time allows.

Heat the oven to 150 °C fan. Place the pork fat-side up in a roasting tin with {{apple-cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar in the base. Roast uncovered for 3 hours, then cover tightly with foil and cook for a further 3–4 hours until the thermometer reads 95 °C at the thickest point. Rest covered for at least 30 minutes.
Pull the pork into fibres using two forks, discarding fat and bone. Season with the resting juices and a pinch of salt. Serve in rolls with coleslaw.
